content.text: A compression-derived algorithm turned foundational NLP technique, Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) builds a vocabulary of subword units by iteratively merging the most frequent adjacent token pairs in a corpus. It sits at the input boundary of every major language model — GPT-2, GPT-4, LLaMA, Falcon, and their successors all rely on BPE or a close variant to convert raw text into the integer sequences that transformers consume.
What it is

BPE was introduced to data compression by Gage (1994) and adapted for neural machine translation by Sennrich et al. (2016). Rather than tokenising text at the word or character level alone, it learns a vocabulary of subword units from the training corpus. The vocabulary size
is a deliberate hyperparameter — typical values range from 30 000 to 100 000 tokens — determined by running exactly
merge operations on top of the character alphabet
The result of BPE training is a
merge table

— an ordered list of (A, B) → AB rules — plus the final vocabulary. At inference time the merge table is replayed in the same order on unseen text, producing an identical tokenisation for any string the model was trained on, and a graceful character-level fallback for novel strings it was not. This closed-form reproducibility is a key property: given the same merge table, the same input always produces the same token sequence, regardless of the runtime environment.
Why it matters

Before subword tokenisation, language models faced a fundamental dilemma: word-level vocabularies cannot represent unseen words (OOV problem), while pure character-level models produce excessively long sequences that make long-range attention expensive. BPE collapses this trade-off by finding a middle ground — common words become single tokens, rare or morphologically complex words decompose into recognisable subpieces. The word "tokenisation" might appear as ["token", "isation"], letting the model leverage shared structure with "tokenize", "organisation", and similar stems it has already seen.

For agentic AI systems this matters in several ways. Tool call payloads, structured JSON arguments, code snippets, and URLs all contain long, unusual strings. A vocabulary that handles subword decomposition gracefully keeps sequence lengths predictable and avoids the out-of-vocabulary cliff where a single unusual identifier consumes dozens of character tokens. Context window budgets are finite — GPT-4's 128k context or Claude's 200k context are measured in
tokens

, not words or bytes. Knowing how BPE maps text to tokens is therefore essential for building agents that pack information efficiently into prompts, count tokens accurately for billing, and detect when a context is about to overflow.
Benefits
Open vocabulary:
any Unicode string can be tokenised by falling back to individual bytes or characters, eliminating hard OOV failures entirely
Compression efficiency:
frequent patterns (common words, common code constructs) become single tokens; rare patterns expand gracefully without ballooning sequence length for common inputs
Language-agnostic:
BPE adapts from training data; no explicit morphological rules are needed, so multilingual corpora tokenise coherently without separate per-language dictionaries
Controllable vocabulary size:
vocab size is a single hyperparameter that trades off embedding table memory against average sequence length — a 32k vocab gives shorter sequences than 8k but requires a larger embedding matrix
Deterministic and reversible:
encode and decode operations are bijective given a fixed merge table; there is no ambiguity in reconstruction, unlike some probabilistic tokenisers
Efficient training:
the greedy merge algorithm runs in near-linear time; training a 50k-token BPE vocabulary on a 10 GB corpus is feasible in minutes on a single machine
Wide framework support:
HuggingFace Tokenizers, tiktoken, SentencePiece, and every major ML framework ship battle-tested BPE implementations that are interoperable through standardised file formats
Trade-offs
Context sensitivity absent:

BPE tokenises greedily left-to-right without context; the same character sequence always produces the same tokens regardless of meaning, which can split morphologically related words differently depending on surrounding whitespace
Whitespace sensitivity:
"Token" and " Token" (with a leading space) are usually different tokens in GPT-style BPE, making prompts fragile to capitalisation and spacing at word boundaries
Language imbalance:

training on English-dominant corpora produces a vocabulary that tokenises English efficiently (roughly one token per common word) but fragments low-resource languages into many characters, inflating their effective context length disproportionately
Non-linguistic splits:

numbers, URLs, code identifiers, and special characters often receive idiosyncratic splits that do not align with human intuitions; "2024" might encode as ["20", "24"] and arithmetic confusion in models partly traces to this
Vocabulary lock-in:
the merge table is fixed at training time; adding new domain terminology post-training requires full re-tokenisation and re-training or fine-tuning on a modified vocabulary, which is costly
Adversarial fragility:
subtle Unicode manipulations (zero-width joiners, look-alike characters) can produce unexpected tokenisations that cause prompt injection or bypass content filters without changing visible text
How it works

BPE training proceeds in two phases: vocabulary initialisation and iterative merging. In the initialisation phase, the corpus is pre-tokenised into words (by whitespace or a language-specific splitter), each word is split into its constituent characters, and a special end-of-word marker (commonly
or a space prefix) is appended to every word to preserve word boundary information. Each unique (word, frequency) pair is added to a word frequency table.
In the merge phase, the algorithm counts the frequency of every adjacent symbol pair across the entire corpus. The most frequent pair
is selected, merged into the new symbol
, and the pair counts are updated. The merge rule
is appended to the merge table. This step is repeated until the vocabulary reaches the target size
. The total number of merges performed is:
where
is the size of the initial character alphabet. For a typical Latin-script corpus
(byte-level BPE uses exactly 256 bytes), so reaching
requires approximately 49 744 merge operations.
At inference time the learned merge table is applied greedily in the
same order

it was learned. Starting from the character-split string, the first merge rule is searched and applied wherever the pair appears, then the second rule, and so on through the entire table. This greedy replay means the tokenisation at inference is deterministic and produces the same segmentation as if the merge had been learned from this exact string. The time complexity of inference for a string of length
is
where
is the number of merges, though fast implementations using priority queues reduce this substantially in practice.
Byte-level BPE

GPT-2 introduced byte-level BPE, where the initial alphabet is all 256 byte values rather than Unicode characters. This guarantees that any input string — any Unicode, any binary — can be tokenised without an unknown token, because the byte-level fallback is always available. The 256 initial byte tokens are always in the vocabulary; merges then build common multi-byte sequences on top of them. This is the variant used by GPT-3, GPT-4, and tiktoken.
Architecture
The BPE pipeline has two distinct phases — training (offline, done once) and inference (online, done per request). The diagram below shows the training merge loop and the final vocabulary output:

The training loop (Steps 1-4) runs offline on the corpus. The resulting merge table and final vocabulary are serialised to disk. At inference time only the vocabulary and merge table are loaded; the original corpus is not needed.
When to use it
BPE is the correct choice for the following situations:
Training a transformer language model from scratch on a text corpus, especially if the corpus is multilingual or domain-specific (code, biomedical text, legal documents)
Matching an existing pre-trained model's tokeniser exactly — GPT-4, GPT-3.5, GPT-2, and related models require tiktoken BPE; using a different tokeniser produces invalid input IDs
Building token-counting utilities for prompt management in agent systems — counting tokens before sending a request avoids context-length errors and enables accurate cost estimation
Fine-tuning on a new domain where OOV is a concern — BPE handles rare domain terms better than word-piece alternatives when the domain vocabulary is entirely new
Prefer alternatives in these scenarios:
Heavily non-Latin script corpora (Arabic, Chinese, Thai) — SentencePiece with Unigram LM often tokenises more naturally because it models character probabilities rather than greedy frequency
Protein sequence or genomics models — character-level or k-mer tokenisation aligns better with biological structure than statistical pair merges
Applications needing stochastic tokenisation for data augmentation — BPE is deterministic; the SentencePiece BPE dropout extension adds randomness, but at integration complexity cost
Mental model

Think of BPE as building a Morse code for text. Morse code gives the shortest symbol sequences to the most common letters (E is a single dot, T is a single dash). BPE gives the shortest token sequences — ideally one token — to the most common character sequences. Rare sequences that never appeared in training fall back to individual character dots and dashes. The merge table is the codebook: it records, in priority order, which character pairs are common enough to earn their own symbol. At decoding time that codebook is read in reverse to reconstruct the original string exactly.

Another helpful frame: BPE is a lossless compression codec for text, where the codec parameters (merge table) are learned from the corpus statistics rather than from mathematical first principles. The transformer model then processes the compressed representation. Compression ratio is the key metric — BPE achieves roughly 4 characters per token for English prose and 3 characters per token for code, compared to 1 character per token for pure character-level models.
How to implement

The clearest path to understanding BPE is implementing the core algorithm from scratch before using a library. The following implementation covers training, encoding, and decoding. It mirrors the original Sennrich et al. approach with word-frequency counting and end-of-word markers.

"""Minimal BPE implementation (training + inference).

Demonstrates the core algorithm without external dependencies.
"""
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple


def build_vocab(corpus: str) -> Dict[Tuple[str, ...], int]:
    """Split corpus into word-frequency pairs with end-of-word markers."""
    vocab: Dict[Tuple[str, ...], int] = defaultdict(int)
    for word in corpus.strip().split():
        # Represent each word as a tuple of characters + end-of-word marker
        chars = tuple(list(word) + ["</w>"])
        vocab[chars] += 1
    return dict(vocab)


def count_pairs(vocab: Dict[Tuple[str, ...], int]) -> Dict[Tuple[str, str], int]:
    """Count frequency of every adjacent symbol pair across the vocabulary."""
    pairs: Dict[Tuple[str, str], int] = defaultdict(int)
    for symbols, freq in vocab.items():
        for i in range(len(symbols) - 1):
            pairs[(symbols[i], symbols[i + 1])] += freq
    return dict(pairs)


def merge_vocab(
    pair: Tuple[str, str],
    vocab: Dict[Tuple[str, ...], int],
) -> Dict[Tuple[str, ...], int]:
    """Apply one merge rule (pair -> merged_token) to the vocabulary."""
    new_vocab: Dict[Tuple[str, ...], int] = {}
    bigram = " ".join(pair)   # e.g. "l o"
    replacement = "".join(pair)  # e.g. "lo"
    for symbols, freq in vocab.items():
        word = " ".join(symbols)
        word = word.replace(bigram, replacement)
        new_vocab[tuple(word.split())] = freq
    return new_vocab


def train_bpe(
    corpus: str,
    num_merges: int,
) -> Tuple[List[Tuple[str, str]], Dict[str, int]]:
    """
    Train BPE on a corpus.

    Returns:
        merge_rules: ordered list of (A, B) pairs that were merged.
        token_to_id: vocabulary mapping token string -> integer id.
    """
    vocab = build_vocab(corpus)

    # Initialise symbol set from all unique characters
    all_symbols: set = set()
    for word in vocab:
        all_symbols.update(word)

    merge_rules: List[Tuple[str, str]] = []

    for i in range(num_merges):
        pairs = count_pairs(vocab)
        if not pairs:
            break
        # Greedily select the most frequent pair (ties broken by sort for determinism)
        best = max(pairs, key=lambda p: (pairs[p], p))
        vocab = merge_vocab(best, vocab)
        merge_rules.append(best)
        all_symbols.add("".join(best))
        print(f"Merge {i + 1}: {best[0]!r} + {best[1]!r} -> {''.join(best)!r}")

    # Build final vocabulary with integer IDs
    token_to_id = {tok: idx for idx, tok in enumerate(sorted(all_symbols))}
    return merge_rules, token_to_id


# ---- Quick demo ----
corpus = "low low low lower lower newest newest newest newest widest widest"
merge_rules, vocab = train_bpe(corpus, num_merges=10)
print(f"\nVocabulary ({len(vocab)} tokens):", sorted(vocab.keys()))
Encoding new text with learned rules

"""BPE inference: apply the learned merge table to encode new text."""


def encode(
    text: str,
    merge_rules: List[Tuple[str, str]],
    token_to_id: Dict[str, int],
) -> List[int]:
    """
    Tokenise `text` using the learned merge table.

    Steps:
        1. Split text into words; character-split each word.
        2. Apply merge rules in order.
        3. Map each resulting token to its integer ID.
    """
    tokens: List[List[str]] = []
    for word in text.strip().split():
        chars = list(word) + ["</w>"]
        tokens.append(chars)

    # Apply each merge rule in training order
    for rule_a, rule_b in merge_rules:
        merged = rule_a + rule_b
        new_tokens = []
        for word_tokens in tokens:
            result = []
            i = 0
            while i < len(word_tokens):
                if (
                    i < len(word_tokens) - 1
                    and word_tokens[i] == rule_a
                    and word_tokens[i + 1] == rule_b
                ):
                    result.append(merged)
                    i += 2
                else:
                    result.append(word_tokens[i])
                    i += 1
            new_tokens.append(result)
        tokens = new_tokens

    # Flatten and map to IDs; fallback to -1 for unseen tokens
    ids = []
    for word_tokens in tokens:
        for tok in word_tokens:
            ids.append(token_to_id.get(tok, -1))
    return ids


def decode(ids: List[int], id_to_token: Dict[int, str]) -> str:
    """Reconstruct text from token IDs."""
    tokens = [id_to_token[i] for i in ids if i in id_to_token]
    text = "".join(tokens)
    return text.replace("</w>", " ").strip()


# Test encoding
id_to_token = {v: k for k, v in vocab.items()}
test = "low lower"
ids = encode(test, merge_rules, vocab)
print("Input:", test)
print("Token IDs:", ids)
print("Decoded:", decode(ids, id_to_token))
Examples
Step-by-step trace
Given the toy corpus
low low low lower lower
, here is what three merge iterations produce:
Iteration
Best pair
Frequency
Resulting vocab change
(l, o)
adds "lo"
(lo, w)
adds "low"
(low, </w>)
adds "low</w>"
After iteration 3, the word "low" encodes to a single token
, while "lower" still decomposes to
— one token for the common stem and individual characters for the morphological suffix, which will soon form "er" once a later merge captures that pair.
Using tiktoken (GPT-4)

"""Using tiktoken -- the production BPE tokeniser for OpenAI models.

Install: pip install tiktoken
"""
import tiktoken

# Load the cl100k_base encoding used by GPT-4 and GPT-3.5
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

# Encode text
text = "def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:"
tokens = enc.encode(text)
print("Token IDs:", tokens)
print("Token count:", len(tokens))

# Decode back to string
reconstructed = enc.decode(tokens)
assert reconstructed == text, "Round-trip mismatch!"
print("Decoded:", reconstructed)

# Inspect individual token strings
for tok_id in tokens:
    tok_bytes = enc.decode_single_token_bytes(tok_id)
    print(f"  {tok_id:6d}  {tok_bytes!r}")


# Practical: count tokens before sending to the API
def count_tokens(messages: list, model: str = "gpt-4") -> int:
    """Count tokens for a chat completion request."""
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    # Each message adds 3 tokens for role/content framing
    total = sum(
        3 + len(enc.encode(m["role"])) + len(enc.encode(m["content"]))
        for m in messages
    )
    total += 3  # reply priming
    return total


messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Explain byte-pair encoding in two sentences."},
]
print(f"\nEstimated request tokens: {count_tokens(messages)}")
Real-world example

GPT-2's cl100k_base successor (used by GPT-4) was trained on a multi-trillion-token corpus with a target vocabulary of 100 256 tokens. The tokeniser handles English, code, 15+ languages, and arbitrary Unicode through byte-level BPE — no unknown-token ID exists because the 256 raw byte tokens are always available as the fallback tier. The resulting tokenisation achieves roughly 4 characters per token for English prose (a 4:1 compression ratio from characters), 3-3.5 characters per token for code (due to shorter identifiers and symbols), and sometimes only 1-2 characters per token for languages with large character sets like Chinese or Arabic.

In production, tiktoken's Rust implementation encodes approximately 1 million tokens per second on a single CPU core. For a 128k-context model, tokenising the full context window takes under 150 ms — fast enough that tokenisation is rarely a latency bottleneck, though it should be profiled in high-throughput batch-inference pipelines.
Common mistakes
Counting characters instead of tokens:

context-window limits and API costs are in tokens, not characters. A prompt with 10 000 characters typically uses roughly 2 500 tokens, but a code-heavy prompt can use far more per character. Always use the model's tokeniser to count before sending.
Using the wrong encoding for the model:

GPT-2 uses gpt2 (50 256 tokens), GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 use cl100k_base (100 256 tokens), and the o1 family uses o200k_base (200 016 tokens). Applying cl100k_base to a GPT-2 prompt produces different IDs that will corrupt the model's input.
Assuming one word equals one token:

common English words like "the", "of", "to" are single tokens; rare words like "tokenisation" are typically two tokens ("token" + "isation"). Code identifiers often split at camelCase or underscore boundaries in surprising ways.
Truncating at character boundaries:

when trimming a prompt to fit a context window, truncating at a character position and then re-encoding can produce more tokens than the original because the mid-word split creates new tokens. Always truncate at token boundaries.
Ignoring leading spaces:

" hello" (space + hello) and "hello" tokenise differently in GPT-style BPE. When constructing multi-part prompts by string concatenation, omitting or duplicating spaces at join points changes the token sequence and can affect model behaviour.
Training BPE on the test set:
the BPE vocabulary should be derived from training data only. Including test text leaks domain information into the tokeniser and inflates evaluation scores for domain-specific terms.
Best practices

Match the pre-trained model's tokeniser exactly: when fine-tuning, use the same vocabulary file and special tokens as the base model. Changing the tokeniser requires reinitialising or expanding the embedding layer, which effectively discards pre-training.

Apply a pre-tokeniser before BPE: use a language-appropriate pre-tokeniser (regex splitter for English, MeCab for Japanese, SentencePiece's built-in for multilingual) before running BPE merges. This prevents merges from crossing word boundaries in ways that confuse syntax-level patterns.

Use the tokeniser's official special tokens: models define a set of reserved token IDs (BOS, EOS, pad, sep). Always use the tokeniser's API methods rather than hard-coding IDs — IDs change between model versions and loading the tokeniser object keeps the code portable.

Cache encodings for repeated strings: if the same system prompt is sent with every request (a common pattern in agent systems), pre-compute and cache its token IDs to avoid redundant tokenisation overhead at runtime.
When training custom BPE, start from at least 1 GB of representative text: below this threshold the learned merges over-represent rare tokens and produce imbalanced vocabularies.

Set a sensible vocab_size: 30k-50k is enough for single-language models; 100k is the practical floor for multilingual models that should handle 20+ languages competently. Larger vocabularies improve per-language efficiency but increase embedding matrix size linearly.
Performance & scalability
BPE training complexity is approximately
where
is the total number of symbols in the corpus and

is the number of merge operations. The original Python implementation in the Sennrich et al. paper runs a full pair-count scan on every iteration. The HuggingFace Tokenizers Rust library uses an incremental approach that updates only affected pair counts after each merge, reducing the constant factor significantly and enabling vocabulary training on 100 GB corpora in under one hour on a modern workstation.

Inference (encoding) with tiktoken's Rust core runs at approximately 1 million tokens per second per CPU core, which translates to about 4 million characters per second for English prose. For a 128k-token context, encoding time is around 128 ms. Decoding is faster still — it is a simple lookup plus byte-to-string conversion. Both operations are thread-safe and can be parallelised trivially across requests in a serving environment.
The embedding table lookup — the first operation inside the model — scales as
in memory. Doubling vocabulary size from 50k to 100k adds
additional parameters. For a model with
(typical for a 7B parameter model), this is 200 million parameters — roughly 800 MB at bf16 precision — just for the extra embedding rows.
Tooling & ecosystem
The BPE tooling ecosystem has standardised around a small number of libraries that cover training, inference, and interoperability.
tiktoken

(OpenAI, Rust + Python): the reference implementation for GPT-2 through GPT-4 and o1 families. The fastest CPU tokeniser available; vocabulary files are fetched from OpenAI's CDN on first use. Install:
pip install tiktoken
HuggingFace Tokenizers
(Rust + Python): supports BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram; provides a unified Tokenizer API with a BPE trainer, fast batch encoding, and serialisation to
tokenizer.json
. Install:
pip install tokenizers
SentencePiece
(Google, C++ + Python): includes a BPE trainer alongside Unigram LM; widely used for T5, mT5, XLM-R, and LLaMA tokenisers. Produces
.model
files portable to C++, Java, and Go. Install:
pip install sentencepiece
transformers AutoTokenizer
(HuggingFace): wraps any of the above; loading any model from the Hub via
AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
automatically downloads and configures the correct BPE (or other) tokeniser

"""Train a custom BPE tokeniser with HuggingFace Tokenizers library.

Use this when fine-tuning on a specialised domain (medical notes,
legal documents, source code) where the base model's vocabulary is suboptimal.
"""
from tokenizers import Tokenizer
from tokenizers.models import BPE
from tokenizers.trainers import BpeTrainer
from tokenizers.pre_tokenizers import Whitespace
from tokenizers.decoders import BPEDecoder

# --- Build and train ---
tokenizer = Tokenizer(BPE(unk_token="[UNK]"))
tokenizer.pre_tokenizer = Whitespace()

trainer = BpeTrainer(
    vocab_size=32_000,
    min_frequency=2,           # ignore pairs appearing fewer than 2 times
    special_tokens=["[UNK]", "[CLS]", "[SEP]", "[PAD]", "[MASK]"],
    show_progress=True,
)

# Files can be paths to plain-text files, one document per line
files = ["train.txt"]          # replace with your corpus file paths
tokenizer.train(files, trainer)
tokenizer.decoder = BPEDecoder()

# --- Serialise ---
tokenizer.save("custom_bpe_tokenizer.json")

# --- Load and use ---
loaded = Tokenizer.from_file("custom_bpe_tokenizer.json")
output = loaded.encode("Byte-pair encoding works well for code.")
print("Tokens:", output.tokens)
print("IDs:   ", output.ids)
print("Decoded:", loaded.decode(output.ids))

# --- Batch encoding (production pattern) ---
batch = loaded.encode_batch([
    "Low-resource language support is important.",
    "The merge table encodes subword structure.",
])
for enc in batch:
    print(enc.tokens)
Comparison with alternatives
The three dominant subword schemes each make different trade-offs between sequence length, vocabulary balance, and multilingual coverage:
Method
Training objective
Inference
Key strength
Main weakness
Used by
BPE
Greedy frequency merges
Deterministic greedy replay
Simple, fast, open-vocabulary via byte fallback
Context-free splits; whitespace-sensitive
GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA, Falcon
WordPiece
Maximise corpus log-likelihood
Greedy longest-match
Better statistical grounding; uses ## prefix for continuation pieces
Same context-free limit; [UNK] for truly unseen bytes
BERT, DistilBERT, ELECTRA
Unigram LM
Probabilistic vocab pruning (EM)
Viterbi decoding (stochastic option)
Soft tokenisation for data augmentation; better multilingual balance
Slower training; stochastic inference adds non-determinism
T5, mT5, XLM-R (via SentencePiece)
Character-level
None (trivial)
Split at character boundaries
Fully open vocabulary; no OOV; good for spelling and OCR tasks
Very long sequences; expensive attention for long-range dependencies
Canine, ByT5
Security & reliability
Prompt injection via tokenisation quirks

BPE tokenisation is not context-aware, which creates an exploitable surface for adversarial inputs. Zero-width Unicode characters (U+200B ZERO WIDTH SPACE, U+200C ZERO WIDTH NON-JOINER) are invisible to human readers but change how BPE segments surrounding text. An attacker can insert these characters into user-controlled input to cause a word that would normally form a single blocked token (a slur or a command keyword) to split into two innocuous tokens, evading content-filter checks that operate on token IDs.
The mitigation is to normalise and sanitise input
before

tokenisation. Apply Unicode NFC or NFKC normalisation, strip or replace zero-width characters, and run content filtering on the decoded string rather than (or in addition to) the token IDs. NFKC is generally preferred for LLM inputs because it decomposes compatibility characters that BPE would otherwise treat as distinct.
Tokeniser version pinning

The tokeniser vocabulary is part of the model's specification. If the tokeniser version changes (e.g., tiktoken ships a new vocabulary file), the same prompt encodes to different token IDs and the model produces different output — even if the model weights are unchanged. In production agentic systems:
Pin tiktoken's vocabulary files to a specific revision in your dependency lock file; do not auto-update them alongside tiktoken version bumps
Store vocabulary files in version control or a content-addressed artifact store alongside model weights — treat them as part of the model artifact, not as an external dependency
Include tokeniser identity (vocabulary hash) in model version metadata so any change is visible in deployment diff reviews
If context-overflow errors appear suddenly in production after a dependency update, check the tokeniser first — a vocabulary change can silently make previously-safe prompts exceed the context limit
Denial-of-service via adversarial tokenisation

A short string can expand to a disproportionately large number of tokens. Repeated Unicode characters that fall outside the learned BPE merge table (a long sequence of uncommon CJK extension characters on an English-trained tokeniser) tokenise to one byte-level token each, inflating sequence length by up to 4x compared to typical English. This can be used to exhaust context budgets or trigger expensive API calls. Rate-limit on character count before tokenising; apply a maximum character budget (e.g., 4x the token limit) as a fast pre-filter.
Key takeaways

BPE builds a vocabulary by iteratively merging the most frequent adjacent character pair in a corpus, repeating until the vocabulary reaches the target size; the merge table is then replayed in order to encode new text
Byte-level BPE (used by GPT-2, GPT-4, tiktoken) starts from all 256 bytes, guaranteeing an open vocabulary with no unknown tokens for any Unicode input
Context windows and API costs are measured in tokens, not characters; always use the model's tokeniser to count before sending a request or before truncating a prompt
BPE is deterministic and reversible — the same input always produces the same token IDs given the same merge table — making it safe to use in reproducible pipelines

Leading whitespace, Unicode normalisation, and zero-width characters all change tokenisation; normalise inputs with NFKC before tokenising and run content filtering on the decoded string, not only on token IDs
When fine-tuning, use the base model's original tokeniser unchanged; swapping or expanding the vocabulary invalidates pre-trained embeddings and substantially raises the cost of adaptation

For production systems, pin vocabulary files by hash alongside model weights, rate-limit on character count before tokenising, and treat tokeniser version changes with the same care as model weight updates
